text:
I've always thought that exact thing could happen in those bottomless pools. It's heavy enough sitting on an upper concrete floor. Great movie! It never stopped. Loved the creativity. It reminded me of the Indian movie Mom with the ordinary woman stalking her prey and doing odd things like grinding up apple seeds. I don't have any criticism of the movie, it was pretty much perfection. 
Loved the foreign agent's talk about filling the silence. I figured as much about the reason for the end scenario and did notice his methods were precisely what he was accused of. Good job on this two hours of entertainment! 
The only flaw in the plot, or rather the protagonist's plan, was that in righting the wrong he created more loose ends and didn't seem to care about fixing those. One in particular. And although the last clip was fun, it really had nothing to do with the film. Oh, wait, below ground with no view and fresh air. Okay, fine. What I didn't understand is if his top secret job was intelligence, work computers in the building should know all his connections, no? Printer? Removing classified downloaded photocopies? If you get away with removing documents from the basement of intelligence offices then you must have latitude to do that in the first place and not need to hide the papers, stuffing them in stealth. Either or. In your most basic civilian job they won't let you go to unsanctioned sites or use electronics to take notes.

text:
Every modern Hollywood film now ends with the protagonist triumphing through sheer self-belief—no training, no preparation, just abstract conviction. Once noticed, it becomes impossible to ignore. It’s formulaic delusion dressed as inspiration.

This narrative crutch has subtly conditioned entire generations to internalize the idea that belief alone is tantamount to ability—that if something feels true, it must be. It’s a seductive fantasy, but dangerously unrealistic. Reality doesn’t bend to sentiment; it yields only to discipline, resilience, and skill.

The greatest action films endure because they respect this principle. The Dark Knight, Bourne, Terminator, Planet of the Apes (Matt Reeves)—these stories are grounded, their worlds coherent, their stakes tangible. The protagonists are not sanctified by wishful thinking but forged through adversity. They are competent, fallible, and fully accountable.

These films resonate because they reflect a reality where triumph must be earned, not imagined. Hollywood has abandoned that ethos. Culture, unsurprisingly, followed.
